sourceQuestion:
Consider the virtual page reference string:
1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 1, 3, 2, 4, 1
on a demand paged virtual memory system running on a computer system that has main memory size of 3 page frames which are initially empty. Let LRU, FIFO and OPTIMAL denote the number of page faults under the corresponding page replacement policy. Then

A. OPTIMAL < LRU < FIFO
B. OPTIMAL < FIFO < LRU
C. OPTIMAL = LRU
D. OPTIMAL = FIFO

parsed.stem:
Consider the virtual page reference string:
1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 1, 3, 2, 4, 1
on a demand paged virtual memory system running on a computer system that has main memory size of 3 page frames which are initially empty. Let LRU, FIFO and OPTIMAL denote the number of page faults under the corresponding page replacement policy. Then

sourceQuestion:
A computer has twenty physical page frames which contain pages numbered 101 through 120. Now a program accesses the pages numbered 1, 2, ..., 20 in that order, and repeats the access sequence THRICE. Which one of the following page replacement policies experiences the same number of page faults as the optimal page replacement policy for this program?

A. Last-in-first-out
B. First-in-first-out
C. Least-recently-used
D. Most-recently-used

parsed.stem: A computer has twenty physical page frames which contain pages numbered 101 through 120. Now a program accesses the pages numbered 1, 2, ..., 20 in that order, and repeats the access sequence THRICE. Which one of the following page replacement policies experiences the same number of page faults as the optimal page replacement policy for this program?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a main memory with five page frames and the following sequence of page references:
3, 8, 2, 3, 9, 1, 6, 3, 8, 2, 3, 6, 2, 1, 3
Which one of the following is true with respect to page replacement policies First In First Out (FIFO) and Least Recently Used (LRU)?

A. Both incur the same number of page faults
B. FIFO incurs more page faults than LRU
C. LRU incurs more page faults than FIFO
D. Both incur the same number of page faults but less than optimal
sourceAnswer: B. FIFO incurs more page faults than LRU

parsed.stem:
Consider a main memory with five page frames and the following sequence of page references:
3, 8, 2, 3, 9, 1, 6, 3, 8, 2, 3, 6, 2, 1, 3
Which one of the following is true with respect to page replacement policies First In First Out (FIFO) and Least Recently Used (LRU)?

options.text: Both incur the same number of page faults
FIFO incurs more page faults than LRU
LRU incurs more page faults than FIFO
Both incur the same number of page faults but less than optimal

sourceQuestion:
Consider a computer system with ten physical page frames. The system is provided with an access sequence (a_1, a_2, ..., a_p, b_1, b_2, ..., b_q), where each a_i and b_j is a distinct virtual page number. The difference in the number of page faults between the last-in-first-out (LIFO) page replacement policy and the optimal page replacement policy is

A. p
B. q
C. p - 10
D. 0

parsed.stem: Consider a computer system with ten physical page frames. The system is provided with an access sequence (a_1, a_2, ..., a_p, b_1, b_2, ..., b_q), where each a_i and b_j is a distinct virtual page number. The difference in the number of page faults between the last-in-first-out (LIFO) page replacement policy and the optimal page replacement policy is

sourceQuestion:
In which one of the following page replacement algorithms is it possible for the page fault rate to increase even when the number of allocated frames increases?
A. LRU (Least Recently Used)
B. OPT (Optimal Page Replacement)
C. MRU (Most Recently Used)
D. FIFO (First In First Out)

parsed.stem: In which one of the following page replacement algorithms is it possible for the page fault rate to increase even when the number of allocated frames increases?

sourceQuestion:
Recall that Belady's anomaly is that the page-fault rate may increase as the number of allocated frames increases. Now, consider the following statements:
S1: Random page replacement algorithm (where a page chosen at random is replaced) suffers from Belady's anomaly.
S2: LRU page replacement algorithm suffers from Belady's anomaly.
Which of the following is CORRECT?
A. S1 is true, S2 is true
B. S1 is true, S2 is false
C. S1 is false, S2 is true
D. S1 is false, S2 is false
sourceAnswer: B. S1 is true, S2 is false

parsed.stem:
Recall that Belady's anomaly is that the page-fault rate may increase as the number of allocated frames increases. Now, consider the following statements:
S1: Random page replacement algorithm (where a page chosen at random is replaced) suffers from Belady's anomaly.
S2: LRU page replacement algorithm suffers from Belady's anomaly.
Which of the following is CORRECT?

options.text: S1 is true, S2 is true
S1 is true, S2 is false
S1 is false, S2 is true
S1 is false, S2 is false

sourceQuestion:
In the context of operating systems, which of the following statements is/are correct with respect to paging?
A. Paging helps solve the issue of external fragmentation
B. Page size has no impact on internal fragmentation
C. Paging incurs memory overheads
D. Multi-level paging is necessary to support pages of different sizes

parsed.stem: In the context of operating systems, which of the following statements is/are correct with respect to paging?

options.text: Paging helps solve the issue of external fragmentation
Page size has no impact on internal fragmentation
Multi-level paging is necessary to support pages of different sizes

sourceQuestion:
Consider a demand paging system with three frames, and the following page reference string:
1 2 3 4 5 4 1 6 4 5 1 3 2
The contents of the frames are as follows initially and after each reference (from left to right):

initially | 1* | 2* | 3* | 4* | 5* | 4  | 1  | 6* | 4  | 5  | 1  | 3* | 2*
---------   --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --
  -        |  1 |  1 |  1 |  4 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  3 |  2
  -        |  - |  2 |  2 |  2 |  2 |  2 |  1 |  1 |  4 |  4 |  1 |  1 |  1
  -        |  - |  - |  3 |  3 |  3 |  4 |  4 |  6 |  6 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5

The *-marked references cause page replacements. Which one or more of the following statements are TRUE?
A. The page replacement policy used could be LRU.
B. The page replacement policy used could be FIFO.
C. The page replacement policy used could be Optimal.
D. The page replacement policy used could be MRU (Most Recently Used).

parsed.stem:
Consider a demand paging system with three frames, and the following page reference string:
1 2 3 4 5 4 1 6 4 5 1 3 2
The contents of the frames are as follows initially and after each reference (from left to right):

initially | 1* | 2* | 3* | 4* | 5* | 4  | 1  | 6* | 4  | 5  | 1  | 3* | 2*
---------   --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --    --
  -        |  1 |  1 |  1 |  4 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  3 |  2
  -        |  - |  2 |  2 |  2 |  2 |  2 |  1 |  1 |  4 |  4 |  1 |  1 |  1
  -        |  - |  - |  3 |  3 |  3 |  4 |  4 |  6 |  6 |  5 |  5 |  5 |  5

The *-marked references cause page replacements. Which one or more of the following statements are TRUE?

options.text: The page replacement policy used could be LRU.
The page replacement policy used could be FIFO.
The page replacement policy used could be Optimal.
The page replacement policy used could be MRU (Most Recently Used).

sourceQuestion:
The address sequence generated by tracing a particular program executing in a pure demand paging system with 100 bytes per page is:
0100, 0200, 0430, 0499, 0510, 0530, 0560, 0120, 0220, 0240, 0410, 0530
Suppose that the memory can store only one page at a time. The number of page faults is:
A. 6
B. 7
C. 8
D. 9

parsed.stem:
The address sequence generated by tracing a particular program executing in a pure demand paging system with 100 bytes per page is:
0100, 0200, 0430, 0499, 0510, 0530, 0560, 0120, 0220, 0240, 0410, 0530
Suppose that the memory can store only one page at a time. The number of page faults is:

sourceQuestion:
A demand paging system takes 100 time units to service a page fault and 300 time units to replace a dirty page. Memory access time is 1 time unit. The probability of a page fault is p. In case of a page fault, the probability of the page being dirty is also p. It is observed that the average memory access time is 3 time units. Then the value of p is:

A. 0.194
B. 0.233
C. 0.514
D. 0.981

parsed.stem: A demand paging system takes 100 time units to service a page fault and 300 time units to replace a dirty page. Memory access time is 1 time unit. The probability of a page fault is p. In case of a page fault, the probability of the page being dirty is also p. It is observed that the average memory access time is 3 time units. Then the value of p is:

sourceQuestion:
Assume that a main memory with only 4 pages, each of 512 bytes, is initially empty. The CPU generates the following sequence of virtual addresses (in decimal):

0, 4, 25, 36, 41, 12, 08, 72, 80, 84, 29, 32, 88, 52

The system uses Least Recently Used (LRU) page replacement policy. How many page faults does this sequence cause? What are the page numbers of the pages present in the main memory at the end of the sequence?

A. 6 and 1, 2, 3, 4
B. 7 and 1, 2, 4, 5
C. 8 and 1, 2, 4, 6
D. 9 and 1, 2, 3, 5

parsed.stem:
Assume that a main memory with only 4 pages, each of 512 bytes, is initially empty. The CPU generates the following sequence of virtual addresses (in decimal):

0, 4, 25, 36, 41, 12, 08, 72, 80, 84, 29, 32, 88, 52

The system uses Least Recently Used (LRU) page replacement policy. How many page faults does this sequence cause? What are the page numbers of the pages present in the main memory at the end of the sequence?

sourceQuestion:
A given set of processes can be implemented by using only parbegin/parend statements, if the precedence graph of these processes is ______

A. a tree
B. a directed acyclic graph
C. a series-parallel graph
D. a complete bipartite graph

parsed.stem: A given set of processes can be implemented by using only parbegin/parend statements, if the precedence graph of these processes is ______

sourceQuestion:
Draw the precedence graph for the concurrent program given below:

S1
parbegin
  begin S2; S4 end;
  begin
    S3;
    parbegin
      S5;
      S6
    parend;
    S7
  end
parend;
S8

Which of the following precedence graphs correctly represents the above program?
A. S1 -> S2 -> S4, S1 -> S3 -> S5 -> S7 -> S8, S3 -> S6 -> S7, S4 -> S8
B. S1 -> S2 -> S4 -> S8, S1 -> S3 -> S5 -> S7 -> S8, S1 -> S6 -> S7
C. S1 -> S2 -> S4, S1 -> S3, S3 -> S5, S3 -> S6, S5 -> S7, S6 -> S7, S4 -> S8, S7 -> S8
D. S1 -> S2, S1 -> S3, S2 -> S4, S3 -> S5, S3 -> S6, S4 -> S8, S5 -> S8, S6 -> S8

The process state transition diagram in the below figure is representative of

(The diagram shows states: Running, Ready, Blocked, and transitions including: Running -> Ready (preemption), Ready -> Running (dispatch), Running -> Blocked (I/O wait), Blocked -> Ready (I/O complete), and notably NO direct path from Running to Exit/Terminated)

A. a batch operating system
B. an operating system with a preemptive scheduler
C. an operating system with a non-preemptive scheduler
D. a uni-programmed operating system
sourceAnswer: B. an operating system with a preemptive scheduler

parsed.stem:
The process state transition diagram in the below figure is representative of

(The diagram shows states: Running, Ready, Blocked, and transitions including: Running -> Ready (preemption), Ready -> Running (dispatch), Running -> Blocked (I/O wait), Blocked -> Ready (I/O complete), and notably NO direct path from Running to Exit/Terminated)

options.text: an operating system with a preemptive scheduler
an operating system with a non-preemptive scheduler

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following does NOT interrupt a running process?

A. A device
B. Timer
C. Scheduler process
D. Power failure

parsed.stem: Which of the following does NOT interrupt a running process?

sourceQuestion:
Which combination of the following features will suffice to characterize an OS as a multi-programmed OS?

a. More than one program may be loaded into main memory at the same time for execution.
b. If a program waits for certain events such as I/O, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.
c. If the execution of a program terminates, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.

A. (a)
B. (a) and (b)
C. (a) and (c)
D. (a), (b) and (c)

parsed.stem:
Which combination of the following features will suffice to characterize an OS as a multi-programmed OS?

a. More than one program may be loaded into main memory at the same time for execution.
b. If a program waits for certain events such as I/O, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.
c. If the execution of a program terminates, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.

sourceQuestion:
Which one or more of the following need to be saved on a context switch from one thread (T1) of a process to another thread (T2) of the same process?
A. Page table base register
B. Stack pointer
C. Program counter
D. General purpose registers

parsed.stem: Which one or more of the following need to be saved on a context switch from one thread (T1) of a process to another thread (T2) of the same process?

sourceQuestion:
The process state transition diagram of an operating system is as given below. (The diagram shows states: Created -> Ready -> Running -> Blocked -> Ready, Running -> Exit, and notably there is NO direct transition from Running back to Ready, i.e., no preemption arrow from Running to Ready.)
Which of the following must be FALSE about the above operating system?
A. It is a multiprogrammed operating system
B. It uses preemptive scheduling
C. It uses non-preemptive scheduling
D. It is a multi-user operating system

parsed.stem:
The process state transition diagram of an operating system is as given below. (The diagram shows states: Created -> Ready -> Running -> Blocked -> Ready, Running -> Exit, and notably there is NO direct transition from Running back to Ready, i.e., no preemption arrow from Running to Ready.)
Which of the following must be FALSE about the above operating system?

options.text: It is a multiprogrammed operating system
It is a multi-user operating system

sourceQuestion:
Assume that the following jobs are to be executed on a single processor system:

Job Id | CPU Burst Time
  p    |      4
  q    |      1
  r    |      3
  s    |      1
  f    |      ?

The jobs are assumed to have arrived at time 0 and in the order p, q, r, s, f. Calculate the departure time (completion time) for job p if scheduling is round robin with time slice 2.

A. 4
B. 10
C. 11
D. 12
E. None of the above

parsed.stem:
Assume that the following jobs are to be executed on a single processor system:

Job Id | CPU Burst Time
  p    |      4
  q    |      1
  r    |      3
  s    |      1
  f    |      ?

The jobs are assumed to have arrived at time 0 and in the order p, q, r, s, f. Calculate the departure time (completion time) for job p if scheduling is round robin with time slice 2.

sourceQuestion:
Which scheduling policy is most suitable for a time shared operating system?

A. Shortest Job First
B. Round Robin
C. First Come First Serve
D. Elevator

parsed.stem: Which scheduling policy is most suitable for a time shared operating system?

sourceQuestion:
The sequence __________ is an optimal non-preemptive scheduling sequence for the following jobs which leaves the CPU idle for __________ unit(s) of time.

Job | Arrival Time | Burst Time
 1  |     0.0      |     3
 2  |     1.0      |     5
 3  |     3.0      |     2

A. (3,2,1), 1
B. (2,1,3), 0
C. (3,2,1), 0
D. (1,2,3), 5

parsed.stem:
The sequence __________ is an optimal non-preemptive scheduling sequence for the following jobs which leaves the CPU idle for __________ unit(s) of time.

Job | Arrival Time | Burst Time
 1  |     0.0      |     3
 2  |     1.0      |     5
 3  |     3.0      |     2

sourceQuestion:
Four jobs to be executed on a single processor system arrive at time 0 in the order A, B, C, D. Their CPU time requirements are 4, 1, 8, 1 time units respectively. The completion time of A under round robin scheduling with time slice of one time unit is:

A. 10
B. 4
C. 8
D. 9

parsed.stem: Four jobs to be executed on a single processor system arrive at time 0 in the order A, B, C, D. Their CPU time requirements are 4, 1, 8, 1 time units respectively. The completion time of A under round robin scheduling with time slice of one time unit is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider n processes sharing the CPU in a round-robin fashion. Assuming that each process switch takes s seconds, what must be the quantum size q such that the overhead resulting from process switching is minimized but at the same time each process is guaranteed to get its turn at the CPU at least every t seconds?

A. q >= s(t-1)/n
B. q >= s(t-s)/(n-1)
C. q <= t/n
D. q >= t(s-1)/(n-1)

parsed.stem: Consider n processes sharing the CPU in a round-robin fashion. Assuming that each process switch takes s seconds, what must be the quantum size q such that the overhead resulting from process switching is minimized but at the same time each process is guaranteed to get its turn at the CPU at least every t seconds?

sourceQuestion:
In a computer system where the 'best-fit' algorithm is used for allocating 'jobs' to 'memory partitions', the following situation was encountered:

Partitions size in KB: 4K  5K  8K  20K  14K  3K  2K
Job sizes in KB:       2K  11K  3K  6K  10K  20K  2K
Time for execution:    4   10   2   1    4    8    6

When will the 20K job complete?

A. 1
B. 10
C. 14
D. 6

parsed.stem:
In a computer system where the 'best-fit' algorithm is used for allocating 'jobs' to 'memory partitions', the following situation was encountered:

Partitions size in KB: 4K  5K  8K  20K  14K  3K  2K
Job sizes in KB:       2K  11K  3K  6K  10K  20K  2K
Time for execution:    4   10   2   1    4    8    6

When will the 20K job complete?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following scheduling algorithms is non-preemptive?

A. Round Robin
B. First-In First-Out
C. Multilevel Queue Scheduling
D. Multilevel Queue Scheduling with Feedback

parsed.stem: Which of the following scheduling algorithms is non-preemptive?

sourceQuestion:
A uni-processor computer system only has two processes, both of which alternate CPU bursts with I/O bursts. Both the processes were created at nearly the same time. The I/O of both processes can proceed in parallel. Which of the following scheduling strategies will result in the least CPU utilization (over a long period of time)?

A. First come first served scheduling
B. Shortest remaining time first scheduling
C. Static priority scheduling with different priorities for the two processes
D. Round robin scheduling with a time quantum of 1 ms
sourceAnswer: A. First come first served scheduling

parsed.stem: A uni-processor computer system only has two processes, both of which alternate CPU bursts with I/O bursts. Both the processes were created at nearly the same time. The I/O of both processes can proceed in parallel. Which of the following scheduling strategies will result in the least CPU utilization (over a long period of time)?

options.text: Static priority scheduling with different priorities for the two processes
Round robin scheduling with a time quantum of 1 ms

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following set of processes, with the arrival times and the CPU-burst times given in milliseconds.

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P1      |      0       |     7
P2      |      1       |     3
P3      |      2       |     1
P4      |      3       |     2

What is the average turnaround time for these processes with the preemptive shortest remaining processing time first (SRPT) algorithm?

A. 4.50
B. 5.75
C. 6.00
D. 6.25

parsed.stem:
Consider the following set of processes, with the arrival times and the CPU-burst times given in milliseconds.

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P1      |      0       |     7
P2      |      1       |     3
P3      |      2       |     1
P4      |      3       |     2

What is the average turnaround time for these processes with the preemptive shortest remaining processing time first (SRPT) algorithm?

sourceQuestion:
Consider three CPU-intensive processes, which require 10, 20, and 30 time units and arrive at times 0, 2, and 6, respectively. How many context switches are needed if the operating system implements a shortest remaining time first scheduling algorithm? Do not count the context switches at time zero and at the end.

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

parsed.stem: Consider three CPU-intensive processes, which require 10, 20, and 30 time units and arrive at times 0, 2, and 6, respectively. How many context switches are needed if the operating system implements a shortest remaining time first scheduling algorithm? Do not count the context switches at time zero and at the end.

sourceQuestion:
Consider three processes (process id 0, 1, 2 respectively) with compute time bursts 2, 4, and 8 time units. All processes arrive at time zero. Consider the longest remaining time first (LRTF) scheduling algorithm. In LRTF ties are broken by giving priority to the process with the lowest process id. The average turn around time is:

A. 13 units
B. 14 units
C. 15 units
D. 16 units

parsed.stem: Consider three processes (process id 0, 1, 2 respectively) with compute time bursts 2, 4, and 8 time units. All processes arrive at time zero. Consider the longest remaining time first (LRTF) scheduling algorithm. In LRTF ties are broken by giving priority to the process with the lowest process id. The average turn around time is:
